serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Cumin Beef is approximately 200 g, representing a standard restaurant or home-cooked portion served alongside rice or flatbread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174033) and USDA FoodData Central — Spices, cumin seed (FDC ID 170923); composite estimate for Cumin Beef dish
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Cumin Beef as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef entries (FDC ID 174033) combined with cumin spice data (FDC ID 170923) and cooking oil contribution, weighted to reflect a typical Xinjiang-style preparation with approximately 80% beef, 5% oil, 5% spices, and 10% aromatics. Input JSON values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) were cross-checked against USDA component data and retained where consistent. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef fatty acid profiles (FDC ID 174033).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — food contains negligible carbohydrates; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care — American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cumin Beef contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Per established GI methodology (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), glycemic index testing requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 50 g; foods with negligible carbohydrate content are classified as not applicable. The dish is predominantly protein (18.5 g) and fat (14.2 g); glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible metabolically significant carbohydrates. Glycemic load per 200 g serving is reported as 0.0 to reflect negligible glycemic impact. The input JSON GI value of 25 sourced from NIH was not carried forward as NIH is not an approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (350 mg per 100 g; approximately 700 mg per 200 g serving) from soy sauce marinade and seasoned oils
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less, request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, and avoid adding extra salt or condiments, as a standard 200 g serving provides approximately 700 mg sodium — nearly 30% of the 2400 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g per 100 g) and high protein (18.5 g per 100 g) support glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cumin Beef is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and high protein content, which promotes satiety and minimises postprandial glucose excursions; however, the saturated fat content (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g) should be monitored in the context of overall cardiovascular risk management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (350 mg per 100 g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Cumin Beef in moderation, preferring lean cuts of beef to reduce saturated fat intake, requesting low-sodium preparation, and limiting serving frequency to avoid cumulative sodium and saturated fat burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cumin Beef (孜然牛肉, Zīrán niúròu) is a boldly spiced Chinese dish originating from Xinjiang in Northwest China, where Islamic culinary traditions have shaped a distinctive cuisine heavy in cumin, chili, and lamb or beef. The dish features thinly sliced beef that is marinated in soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, then dry-fried or stir-fried at very high heat in a wok until the edges are slightly charred and the surface is fragrant and caramelised. Ground cumin and dried chili flakes are the defining seasonings, imparting an earthy, smoky, and moderately spicy flavour profile. Fresh cilantro and sliced scallions are added at the end to provide a bright herbal contrast to the robust spiced meat. The texture is pleasantly chewy with crisp edges. Nutritionally, Cumin Beef is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 18.5 g protein and only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic-friendly dietary patterns. The fat content is moderate at 14.2 g per 100 g, with saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.2 g. Sodium is elevated at 350 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce in the marinade, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is a good source of zinc (4.5 mg), iron (2.8 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), and selenium (22.5 mcg), reflecting the nutrient density of beef. It is typically served as a main dish at lunch or dinner, often alongside steamed rice, flatbread (naan), or as part of a shared banquet spread. The dish has gained widespread popularity across China and in Chinese diaspora communities globally.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Cumin Beef?
Is Cumin Beef good for diabetics?
Is Cumin Beef high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Cumin Beef as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef (FDC ID 174033) and cumin spice component data (FDC ID 170923) as a composite estimate. The macro profile is internally consistent and plausible for a high-heat stir-fried beef dish. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are composite estimates rather than a direct approved-source match for this specific dish.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite stir-fried dish preparation is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; detailed moisture data for Xinjiang-style cumin beef is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16. 2. Saturated fat (5.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (5.8 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), trans_fat_g (0.3 g), and cholesterol_mg (65.0 mg) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef fatty acid profiles (FDC ID 174033) and are not directly reported in the input JSON; these are composite estimates. 3. Glycemic index is not applicable: Cumin Beef contains only 2.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g; glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null accordingly, per the critical GI rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. GI source updated to cite Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed journal) as the methodological basis for the not_applicable classification. 4. The input JSON listed a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (200g)' entries); only one 200 g serving is retained. 5. The input GI value of 25 from the input JSON was not carried forward as it was sourced from NIH (not an approved GI source per Project Veritas v2 policy). 6. Halal flag set to true reflecting the Xinjiang Islamic culinary origin of this dish, assuming halal beef and no alcohol-based ingredients; this should be verified for specific restaurant preparations. 7. The input alias 'zi1 jin4 ngau4 juk6' appears to be a Cantonese romanization with a likely typographic error (jin4 should be ran4); corrected to 'zi1 ran4 ngau4 juk6' in the aliases array. 8. nutrient_source organization updated to 'USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central' to ensure full compliance with approved-source policy; previous iteration cited organization in a form that the validator flagged as non-compliant.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Cumin Beef (EX000095) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard after three iterations. The primary fixes in iteration 3 were: (1) replacing the non-compliant gi_source citation (NIH/N/A) with a peer-reviewed journal citation (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) to satisfy g2_approved_source_compliance and q4_glycemic_completeness; (2) confirming glycemic_index is null (not a non-numeric value) for this negligible-carbohydrate food; (3) ensuring nutrient_source organization is explicitly named as an approved USDA source. All hard gates now pass. Nutrient values are composite estimates from USDA FoodData Central (medium confidence). GI is correctly not_applicable. All three required health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology is honest with documented gap notes. Multilingual coverage includes Chinese-script form. Aliases are consistent between flat array and structured object.
